title: Treasure Hunt Engine: From Default Config to Production Ready: What the Docs Do Not Say
author: Veltrix
excerpt: A practical breakdown of the Veltrix configuration layer that determines whether your server scales cleanly or stalls at the first growth inflection point.

content.text: Operators running Hytale servers with the Veltrix toolkit consistently reach the topic of treasure hunt engine at the same point in their server lifecycle — not during initial setup, but two to four weeks after launch when real player behavior has exposed the gaps in their Veltrix configuration. The default Veltrix configuration files ship with values calibrated for demonstration and testing environments. They are not production-calibrated. This guide addresses the specific Veltrix configuration decisions that determine whether the treasure hunt engine layer of your stack performs correctly under real player load, and what to adjust when it does not.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

The search cluster around events configuration in Veltrix reveals a consistent pattern of operators hitting production limitations that the documentation did not prepare them for. Operators reach this topic after initial deployment, when the Veltrix default configuration has been running long enough for real player behavior to reveal its production limitations. The questions that emerge are not about how the configuration works — they are about why it is not working the way the Veltrix documentation implied it would at real player counts.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

The success of a Treasure Hunt on a Veltrix‑managed Hytale server hinges on how the events.yml is wired. Setting `eventType: treasure_hunt` triggers the internal Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01, but the magic lies in the nested keys that dictate participant limits and cliff edges. A `maxClues` value of 60 coupled with a `clueSpawnDelay` of 15 seconds imposes a brisk pace that keeps players on the edge of curiosity, while a too‑lenient `maxPlayers` of 200 dilutes leaderboards and reduces the perceived mastery. In prizes.yml, the `rewardPool` must reference tiered items that correlate to `clueConfidence` thresholds; if the pool is too shallow, participants will quickly abandon the hunt for something else. The `spawnPoints` array inside events.yml must balance scarcity against accessibility; uneven distribution encourages exploration but can also frustrate players who feel unlucky, ultimately impacting engagement metrics and diminishing long‑term attendance.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

A well‑timed event calendar invites habitual logins, and Veltrix lets operators choreograph that rhythm through events.yml recurrence schedules and announce.yml timing cues. The `startTime` field, expressed in cron syntax, should be paired with `recurrence: weekly` and a `cooldown: 14h` to prevent spamming while keeping the event feeling fresh. Operators often mis‑set `recurrence` to ` daily`, causing the event to overlap with core spawn times, drowning players in notifications and surfacing cluttered chat. In announce.yml, the `messageTiming` of `5min_before` works best for Treasure Hunts, giving players a heads‑up without stalling the launch. A mis‑configured `frequency` of `always` instead of `once_per_event` leads to repetitive chatter that players find annoying, diminishing perceived value each session.
Seamlessly linking prizes.yml item rarity directly to events.yml difficulty tiers amplifies player commitment—an elegant, low‑effort tweak that instantly rewards consistency.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

The most consistent mistake in Veltrix event configuration is setting events.yml to weekly frequency and expecting it to drive daily retention. Weekly events generate one day of high engagement with no measurable effect on the other six days. The second mistake is setting prize-amount in prizes.yml as an absolute currency value without calibrating against the server earn rate. The third mistake is not configuring the announce.yml three-stage broadcast sequence — single-announcement events have significantly lower participation. The fourth is not logging participation data from the first events to inform configuration of subsequent ones.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Calendar first in events.yml, then announce.yml configuration, then prizes.yml calibration against the established earn rate, then deploy the simplest event type and run it for two full weeks before adding additional event types. The event calendar is the product — individual events are components of it. Do not modify prizes.yml or events.yml based on intuition. Modify them based on participation data logged from the first two weeks of operation.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

In a Veltrix events.yml file the most impact comes from the top‑level timer and participant options. Start by setting eventDuration to a realistic hour range; 120 minutes works for thirty players but caps at 240 minutes for a thousand player roster. The maxParticipants parameter locks the pool and should be 60 for 30 players, 200 for 200 players, and 500 for 500+; any lower and loot cannons finish too early, any higher and server memory leaks. SpawnFrequency controls how many treasure nodes appear per minute; use 0.5 per minute for small games, bump to 1.2 for mid‑scale, and 2.0 for large crowds so the map never feels empty. The lootPoolSize must mirror expected picks: 300 units for small, 800 for medium, and 2000 when the map saturates. Lastly, ensure rewardChance falls between 0.25 for low‑density maps and 0.40 for dense ones to keep the reward rate balanced. These values underline that a one‑size‑fits‑all never works.

When Your Server Starts to Scale

In a Veltrix events.yml file the most impact comes from the timer and participant parameters. Set eventDuration to 120 minutes for a thirty‑player run, extending to 240 minutes when the roster grows to 1,000. The maxParticipants lock is 60 for 30 players, 200 for 200 players, and 500 for over 500. Lower values overrun loot, higher values waste resources. SpawnFrequency defines treasure nodes per minute: use 0.5 for small games, 1.2 for medium, and 2.0 for large audiences so the world never feels empty. The lootPoolSize matches expected picks: 300 units for small, 800 for medium, and 2,000 for saturated maps. RewardChance should range 0.25 for sparse maps to 0.40 for dense ones to keep the reward rate balanced. These settings illustrate that a one‑size‑fits‑all never works.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before launching a Treasure Hunt Engine event on a Veltrix‑managed Hytale server, verify that every critical configuration file reflects production readiness. In events.yml the 'TreasureHunt' event must be marked active, the start_time and end_time must be absolute, past the current timestamp, and prize_tokens must reference a valid identifier in prizes.yml. The prizes.yml file must contain a non‑empty prizes list for this event, each with a correct reward_type, amount, and a chance value that sums to 1.00 when summed across all prizes. announce.yml must include an announce interval that is no longer than 15 minutes, along with a message_template that contains the placeholder {event_name} so players see the correct name. If the event is part of a tournament, tournament.yml must define a bracket with at least two teams, each having a unique UUID in the participants list. The event's schedule must not overlap with any other active event scheduled in events.yml to avoid prize duplication or API conflicts. Lastly, confirm that the server's global notification system is enabled, ensuring that broadcast messages will propagate to all active players when the event starts or ends.

The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
